_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology and burden from Mesothelioma UK, US SEER, WHO/IARC monograph 100C (asbestos). Histology and clinical course per Carbone et al. Nat Rev Dis Primers 2019 (PMID 31748550) and Robinson BWS & Lake RA. NEJM 2005 (PMID 16177252). First-line chemotherapy per Vogelzang et al. JCO 2003 cisplatin-pemetrexed (PMID 12860938). First-line immunotherapy per Baas P et al. NEJM 2021 CheckMate 743 nivolumab-ipilimumab (PMID 33476593). Pleurectomy/decortication vs EPP per Rusch et al. and MARS2 trial Lim E et al. Lancet Respir Med 2024. Peritoneal mesothelioma CRS+HIPEC per Sugarbaker et al. and Yan TD et al. JCO 2009 (PMID 19826129). IASLC 8th-edition staging per Rusch VW et al. J Thorac Oncol 2016 (PMID 27117833). Asbestos exposure-latency data per Lin RT et al. PLoS One 2007 and Driscoll T et al. Am J Ind Med 2005.

h1Title: Mesothelioma: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Mesothelioma is an aggressive cancer of mesothelial cells lining body cavities, in most cases caused by asbestos exposure decades earlier. Pleural mesothelioma accounts for roughly 80% of cases, peritoneal disease for 10-20%, and pericardial or tunica vaginalis tumors for under 1%. Global incidence is approximately 30,000-40,000 cases per year, concentrated in countries with historical heavy asbestos use such as the United Kingdom, Australia, the Netherlands, Italy, and parts of the United States; rising rates are now documented in Russia, China, India, and Brazil. Median age at diagnosis is 65-75 with strong male predominance reflecting occupational exposure decades earlier — typical latency from first asbestos exposure to diagnosis is 30-50 years. The three histological subtypes — epithelioid (60-70%), sarcomatoid (10-20%), and biphasic (10-20%) — predict outcome, with epithelioid disease responding best to therapy. First-line treatment for unresectable pleural disease is now first-line immunotherapy with nivolumab plus ipilimumab (CheckMate 743 demonstrated a median overall survival of 18.1 months versus 14.1 months with cisplatin-pemetrexed) or cisplatin-pemetrexed chemotherapy, with surgery (pleurectomy/decortication) and radiotherapy used in selected patients. Peritoneal mesothelioma treated with cytoreductive surgery plus hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC) achieves median survival of 30-90 months in selected patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 30,000-40,000 new cases per year globally (Carbone Nat Rev Dis Primers 2019); incidence rising in countries with delayed asbestos bans
keyStats.demographics: Male-to-female ratio 4-5:1 in pleural disease (reflecting occupational asbestos exposure); peritoneal disease has more even sex distribution
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at diagnosis 65-75; latency from first asbestos exposure 30-50 years
keyStats.globalCases: Pleural ~80%, peritoneal 10-20%, pericardial and tunica vaginalis <1%; median overall survival 12-21 months in pleural disease and 30-90 months in selected peritoneal disease

definition: Mesothelioma (ICD-10: C45) is a malignant tumor arising from mesothelial cells that line the pleura, peritoneum, pericardium, and tunica vaginalis. It is overwhelmingly caused by inhalation or ingestion of asbestos fibers — chrysotile (white), crocidolite (blue), and amosite (brown) — over years of occupational or environmental exposure, with crocidolite the most potent fibre type per unit dose. The malignant mesothelioma cell carries characteristic genetic alterations including loss of BAP1 (in roughly 60% of cases), CDKN2A deletions, NF2 mutations, and inactivation of the tumor-suppressor genes that normally restrain mesothelial proliferation. The latency from first asbestos exposure to clinical disease is typically 30-50 years, with shorter latency in heavier exposures. Three histological subtypes are recognized: epithelioid (60-70% of pleural cases) with the best prognosis, sarcomatoid (10-20%) with the worst, and biphasic or mixed (10-20%) with intermediate outcomes. Diagnosis requires thoracoscopic, pleural-needle, or peritoneal biopsy with immunohistochemistry showing characteristic positive staining for calretinin, cytokeratin 5/6, WT1, and D2-40 podoplanin, with negative staining for CEA, TTF-1, claudin 4, and BerEP4 (the panel used to distinguish from metastatic adenocarcinoma). The disease is staged by the IASLC TNM (8th edition for pleural and 9th edition in development) with separate staging systems for peritoneal disease. Management is delivered by multidisciplinary teams of thoracic and surgical oncology, medical oncology, radiation oncology, palliative care, occupational medicine, and (often) medico-legal services since most patients qualify for compensation under occupational disease schemes.

typesClassification.description: Accounts for around 80% of cases. Presents with progressive dyspnea, chest pain, pleural effusion, and weight loss. Median overall survival 12-21 months with current systemic therapy; longer with selected surgical and immunotherapy approaches.

10-20% of cases. Presents with abdominal pain, ascites, weight loss, and abdominal distension. Selected patients with low-volume disease confined to peritoneum can achieve median survival of 30-90 months with cytoreductive surgery plus hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC).
Under 1% of cases. Presents with constrictive pericarditis, pericardial effusion, and right heart failure. Very poor prognosis; typically diagnosed late.
Under 1% of cases. Presents as a slow-growing scrotal mass mistaken for hydrocele. Median survival approximately 23 months; treated with orchidectomy and adjuvant therapy in selected cases.

60-70% of pleural mesothelioma. Characterized by cohesive sheets of cuboidal cells with tubulopapillary patterns and best response to therapy. Median survival 18-22 months with modern systemic therapy.
10-20% of pleural mesothelioma. Spindle-cell morphology with worst prognosis (median survival 6-8 months). Limited response to chemotherapy but may respond to immunotherapy.
10-20% of cases. Contains both epithelioid and sarcomatoid components in varying proportions. Prognosis intermediate, depending on the percentage of each component.

primarySymptoms: Insidious onset of breathlessness, initially on exertion and progressing over weeks to months from accumulating pleural effusion compressing the lung.
Persistent unilateral chest pain that is dull, aching, and gradually progressive — often the second most prominent symptom in pleural disease.
Unintentional weight loss of 5-10 kg over 3-6 months, often the symptom that prompts presentation.
Progressive fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance.
Persistent dry cough or hoarseness from local invasion of mediastinal structures.
Abdominal pain, bloating, and ascites in peritoneal mesothelioma, often mistaken for ovarian cancer in women and other intra-abdominal malignancy in men.
Progressive abdominal distension with palpable masses in late peritoneal disease.
Constitutional symptoms — sweats, low-grade fevers, anorexia.
Swelling of the face and arms in superior vena cava syndrome from advanced mediastinal involvement.
Pericardial tamponade — hypotension, distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds — in pericardial mesothelioma.
Painless scrotal swelling that fails to transilluminate in tunica vaginalis mesothelioma; often initially mistaken for hydrocele.
earlyWarningSigns: Recurrent unilateral pleural effusion of unknown cause in someone with possible asbestos exposure decades earlier
Persistent unilateral chest pain without obvious cardiac or musculoskeletal cause in a former shipyard, construction, or insulation worker
Weight loss with progressive dyspnea in a man aged 60-80 with occupational asbestos history
Persistent abdominal pain and ascites in an older adult without classic cancer markers
Pleural plaques or pleural thickening on routine chest imaging in an asbestos-exposed worker — pleural plaques alone are not premalignant but indicate exposure and warrant monitoring
emergencySigns: Acute massive pleural effusion with severe respiratory distress — requires urgent thoracentesis and oxygen
Superior vena cava syndrome with facial and arm swelling, plethora, and headache — needs urgent referral and stenting or radiotherapy
Pericardial tamponade — hypotension, distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds — requires immediate pericardial drainage
Severe pleuritic pain with new onset of fever — exclude empyema and post-thoracentesis infection
Acute spinal cord compression from metastatic disease — requires urgent imaging and corticosteroids

causes.description: Approximately 80% of mesothelioma cases are attributable to asbestos exposure (IARC Group 1 carcinogen). Crocidolite (blue) is the most potent fibre per unit dose, followed by amosite (brown), and then chrysotile (white). Occupational risk is highest in shipyard workers, insulation installers, asbestos miners, asbestos cement manufacturers, plumbers, pipefitters, electricians, boiler workers, naval personnel, demolition workers, and brake-lining manufacturers. Para-occupational exposure occurs in family members of asbestos workers via dust on clothing.

causes.cause: Erionite and other natural fiber exposure
causes.description: Erionite (a fibrous zeolite) is a non-asbestos natural fibre that is even more carcinogenic than asbestos per unit dose. The Anatolian villages of Cappadocia (Turkey) had mesothelioma incidence over 50% attributable to erionite in soil and building materials. Less commonly, fluoro-edenite and other natural fibres have driven local epidemics.

Therapeutic radiation, particularly to the chest or abdomen for previous Hodgkin lymphoma, breast cancer, or other malignancies, increases mesothelioma risk over decades. Treatment-related mesothelioma typically presents in the radiation field 10-30 years after exposure.

causes.cause: Germline BAP1 mutations and other genetic predispositions
causes.description: Germline mutations in BAP1 cause the BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome with elevated risks of mesothelioma, uveal melanoma, renal cell carcinoma, cutaneous melanoma, and other malignancies. BAP1-associated mesothelioma is typically peritoneal, lower-grade, and longer-surviving. Germline testing is offered to younger patients (under 60), those with family history of BAP1-related cancers, or those with peritoneal disease.

SV40 contamination of polio vaccines (1955-1963) was historically proposed as a co-factor in mesothelioma development. Current consensus considers this implausible, with epidemiological studies and contemporary mesothelioma molecular biology favouring asbestos and other established factors.

riskFactors.description: Single most important risk factor. Highest in shipyard, insulation, asbestos cement, mining, demolition, plumbing, boiler, and brake-lining work. Even brief but heavy exposure can cause mesothelioma decades later.
Family members of asbestos workers carry elevated risk from fibres on clothing and shoes. Women laundering work clothes have been documented with significantly elevated mesothelioma rates.

riskFactors.factor: Living near asbestos mines, factories, or naturally occurring asbestos deposits
riskFactors.description: Communities adjacent to asbestos operations (Wittenoom in Australia, Libby in Montana, parts of Quebec, parts of South Africa) have markedly elevated mesothelioma rates.

Male-to-female ratio 4-5:1 in pleural mesothelioma reflects occupational exposure patterns. Female cases more often relate to household exposure, environmental contamination, or germline BAP1 mutations.
Mesothelioma is rare under age 50 because of the long latency from exposure. Median age at diagnosis is 65-75. Pediatric mesothelioma is exceptionally rare.

riskFactors.factor: Prior radiation therapy to the chest or abdomen
riskFactors.description: Therapeutic radiation increases risk decades later, particularly for survivors of Hodgkin lymphoma and breast cancer. Latency 10-30 years.

BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome carries lifetime mesothelioma risk much higher than baseline. Family history of uveal melanoma, renal cell carcinoma, or cutaneous melanoma should raise consideration.

riskFactors.factor: Erionite and other non-asbestos mineral fibers
riskFactors.description: Erionite in soil and stone causes major mesothelioma clusters in central Anatolia, Turkey. Other fibers (taconite, certain talc contamination) are under active investigation.

affectedDemographics: United Kingdom: highest national age-standardised mesothelioma rate globally with around 2,700 deaths/year reflecting heavy 20th-century asbestos use in construction and shipbuilding.
Australia: approximately 700-750 cases/year; Wittenoom in Western Australia produced one of the heaviest documented occupational exposures.
United States: approximately 3,000 cases/year; concentrated in shipbuilding, construction, automotive, and chemical-industry workers.
Italy, Netherlands, Belgium, France, Germany: high rates following decades of industrial asbestos use; many countries banned asbestos by the early 2000s.
Russia, China, India, Brazil, Kazakhstan: rising incidence as countries that continued asbestos use later or have not yet banned it. Russia remains the world's largest producer.
Turkey (Cappadocia and other foci): natural erionite exposure produced mesothelioma incidence approaching 50% in some affected villages.
Female cases: roughly 20-25% of all mesothelioma in regions with strong occupational legislation, more often peritoneal and more often BAP1-related.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of mesothelioma is histopathological: a biopsy demonstrating malignant cells with characteristic immunohistochemistry confirms the diagnosis. The clinical pathway typically begins with persistent symptoms (dyspnea, chest pain, abdominal distension, weight loss), unexplained pleural or peritoneal effusion, or incidental imaging findings. Initial workup includes chest X-ray (often shows pleural thickening or effusion with characteristic shrunken hemithorax), contrast-enhanced CT of chest and abdomen (defines pleural thickening, mediastinal lymphadenopathy, and intra-abdominal disease), and pleural fluid sampling. Pleural fluid cytology has low sensitivity (around 30-40%) for mesothelioma; closed pleural biopsy adds little; video-assisted thoracoscopic (VATS) pleural biopsy is the gold standard with sensitivity over 90%. For peritoneal disease, laparoscopic biopsy with peritoneal cancer index (PCI) assessment is standard. Immunohistochemistry panel for diagnosis: positive markers include calretinin, cytokeratin 5/6, WT1, and D2-40 (podoplanin); negative markers include CEA, TTF-1, claudin 4, and BerEP4. BAP1 immunostaining (loss of nuclear staining) and MTAP (loss of cytoplasmic staining as surrogate for CDKN2A deletion) help distinguish malignant mesothelioma from reactive mesothelial hyperplasia, which can be morphologically indistinguishable. Staging uses IASLC TNM 8th edition for pleural disease and Peritoneal Cancer Index plus completeness of cytoreduction score for peritoneal disease. PET-CT is used to identify distant metastases. Genetic counselling and BAP1 germline testing is offered to younger patients (under 60), those with peritoneal disease, or those with family history of BAP1-related cancers. All confirmed cases should be reviewed at a specialist mesothelioma multidisciplinary team meeting before treatment.

diagnosticTests.test: Contrast-enhanced CT of chest, abdomen, and pelvis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines pleural or peritoneal disease extent, mediastinal lymphadenopathy, and metastases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard CT scan with IV iodinated contrast, takes 15-20 minutes. Result within 24 hours. Identifies characteristic pleural rind and lung trapping.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for tissue diagnosis of pleural mesothelioma — sensitivity >90%
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Day-case or overnight procedure under general anesthesia with chest tube drainage afterwards. Direct visualization and biopsy of parietal pleura; pleurodesis can be performed at the same operation.

diagnosticTests.test: Laparoscopic biopsy with peritoneal cancer index assessment
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for peritoneal mesothelioma diagnosis and staging
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Day-case or overnight procedure under general anesthesia. Allows direct visualization, biopsy, and Sugarbaker PCI scoring. Result within 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: Immunohistochemistry panel (calretinin, CK5/6, WT1, D2-40, CEA, TTF-1, claudin 4, BerEP4, BAP1, MTAP)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms mesothelioma and distinguishes from metastatic carcinoma and reactive mesothelial hyperplasia
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on biopsy specimen by an experienced pathologist; result within 1-2 weeks. BAP1 loss and MTAP loss support malignancy over reactive hyperplasia.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies distant metastases and supports staging
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard PET-CT scan with 18F-FDG tracer; takes 2-3 hours total. Result within 48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome — informs surveillance and family counseling
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood sample sent to a clinical genetics laboratory; result within 2-4 weeks. Offered to younger patients, peritoneal disease, or family history of BAP1-related cancers.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of mesothelioma is delivered by multidisciplinary teams and tailored to histology, disease distribution, performance status, and patient preferences. For pleural mesothelioma, systemic therapy is the cornerstone for most patients because the disease is rarely resectable to negative margins. First-line therapy in 2026 is either combination immunotherapy with nivolumab plus ipilimumab (240 mg nivolumab IV every 2 weeks plus 1 mg/kg ipilimumab IV every 6 weeks for up to 2 years) — particularly favored for non-epithelioid histology — or platinum-pemetrexed chemotherapy (cisplatin 75 mg/m² or carboplatin AUC 5 plus pemetrexed 500 mg/m² IV every 21 days for 4-6 cycles, with vitamin B12 and folic acid prophylaxis) plus bevacizumab in eligible patients. CheckMate 743 demonstrated improved overall survival (18.1 vs 14.1 months) with nivolumab-ipilimumab versus cisplatin-pemetrexed, with particular benefit in non-epithelioid histology. Surgery (pleurectomy/decortication, P/D) is offered to selected fit patients with epithelioid disease and limited tumor burden, often as part of multimodality therapy with chemotherapy and radiotherapy. The recent MARS2 trial showed that extrapleural pneumonectomy (EPP) does not improve survival over P/D and adds morbidity, so EPP is now rarely performed. Adjuvant radiotherapy to the surgical site is sometimes used but does not consistently improve survival. For peritoneal mesothelioma, cytoreductive surgery (CRS) plus hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC) with cisplatin or mitomycin C is standard in selected patients with median survival of 30-90 months in well-selected cohorts. Second-line systemic therapy for relapsed disease includes nivolumab monotherapy, gemcitabine plus ramucirumab, vinorelbine, and clinical trial enrollment. Palliative care should be integrated from diagnosis: management of dyspnea with thoracentesis, indwelling pleural catheter, talc pleurodesis or repeat drainage; pain management with multimodal analgesia and sometimes intercostal nerve blocks or radiation; nutritional support; and psychosocial support for patient and family. Many patients qualify for occupational disease compensation; medico-legal referral should be offered. Genetic counseling and BAP1 testing are offered to younger or familial cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Nivolumab 240 mg IV every 2 weeks plus ipilimumab 1 mg/kg IV every 6 weeks (up to 2 years)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line dual immune checkpoint inhibitor regimen for unresectable pleural mesothelioma, particularly non-epithelioid histology. Side effects include immune-mediated adverse events (colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies) requiring monitoring and corticosteroid management.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median overall survival 18.1 months vs 14.1 months with cisplatin-pemetrexed in CheckMate 743 (Baas NEJM 2021, PMID 33476593); 4-year OS 17% vs 10%

medicalTreatments.name: Cisplatin 75 mg/m² (or carboplatin AUC 5) plus pemetrexed 500 mg/m² IV every 21 days for 4-6 cycles
medicalTreatments.description: First-line chemotherapy regimen with vitamin B12 (1 mg IM every 9 weeks) and folic acid (400-1000 µg PO daily). Adverse effects include nausea, fatigue, neutropenia, nephrotoxicity (cisplatin).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median OS 12.1 months versus 9.3 months with single-agent cisplatin (Vogelzang JCO 2003, PMID 12860938); higher response rate

medicalTreatments.name: Bevacizumab 15 mg/kg IV every 21 days added to platinum-pemetrexed
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-VEGF antibody used as triplet therapy in eligible patients (no recent bleeding, no significant hypertension, no proteinuria). Adds modest survival benefit.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median OS 18.8 vs 16.1 months in MAPS trial (Zalcman Lancet 2016)

medicalTreatments.name: Pemetrexed-platinum plus pembrolizumab (KEYNOTE-483)
medicalTreatments.description: Recently demonstrated improved overall survival over chemotherapy alone in pleural mesothelioma. Alternative first-line option to nivolumab-ipilimumab.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median OS 17.3 vs 16.1 months in KEYNOTE-483; benefit particularly in epithelioid disease

medicalTreatments.name: Nivolumab 240 mg IV every 2 weeks (second-line monotherapy)
medicalTreatments.description: Single-agent immune checkpoint inhibitor for relapsed pleural mesothelioma after prior chemotherapy or in patients unable to tolerate combination therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median OS 9.2 months vs 6.6 months with placebo in CONFIRM trial (Fennell Lancet Oncol 2021)

medicalTreatments.name: Pain management with multimodal analgesia, intercostal nerve blocks, and palliative radiotherapy
medicalTreatments.description: Chest wall pain often requires multimodal approach combining opioids, neuropathic agents, NSAIDs (with caution), and locoregional techniques. Palliative external-beam radiotherapy (20-30 Gy) provides good pain control in chest wall disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction in 50-70% of patients with structured palliative approach

medicalTreatments.name: Indwelling pleural catheter for recurrent pleural effusion
medicalTreatments.description: Tunnelled catheter inserted under local anesthesia allowing intermittent home drainage. Reduces hospital admissions and improves quality of life. Talc pleurodesis is an alternative for trapped lung is the lung re-expands.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic relief in over 90% of recurrent effusion patients; allows outpatient management

surgicalOptions.name: Pleurectomy/decortication (P/D)
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of all visible tumor on parietal and visceral pleura while preserving the lung. Lung-sparing approach now preferred over extrapleural pneumonectomy. Performed at high-volume specialist centres only.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Median OS 21 months in selected fit patients with epithelioid disease and complete cytoreduction in MARS2 and other contemporary series; perioperative mortality under 5% at experienced centres

surgicalOptions.name: Extrapleural pneumonectomy (EPP)
surgicalOptions.description: Historical aggressive surgical approach removing pleura, lung, diaphragm, and pericardium en bloc. The 2024 MARS2 trial demonstrated no survival benefit over P/D plus chemotherapy with greater morbidity, so EPP is now rarely performed.
surgicalOptions.successRate: No survival advantage over P/D; perioperative mortality 4-8% even at expert centres

surgicalOptions.name: Cytoreductive surgery plus hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (CRS+HIPEC)
surgicalOptions.description: Standard for selected patients with peritoneal mesothelioma. Aggressive cytoreduction to no visible residual disease, followed by intraperitoneal heated cisplatin or mitomycin C for 90 minutes intraoperatively.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Median OS 30-90 months in well-selected patients; 5-year survival approaches 50% in epithelioid peritoneal mesothelioma at experienced centres

surgicalOptions.name: Indwelling pleural catheter placement
surgicalOptions.description: Palliative day-case procedure under local anesthesia to manage recurrent pleural effusion. Alternative or complementary to talc pleurodesis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective symptomatic management of recurrent effusion in over 90% of patients; allows outpatient drainage at home

surgicalOptions.name: Talc pleurodesis (chemical or VATS)
surgicalOptions.description: Instillation of sterile talc into the pleural space to produce fibrous obliteration of the pleural space, preventing effusion recurrence in patients with re-expandable lung.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful pleurodesis in 70-90% of patients with re-expandable lung; lower if trapped lung is present

alternativeTreatments.name: Clinical trial enrollment with investigational agents
alternativeTreatments.description: Patients should be considered for trials evaluating novel targeted agents, cellular therapies (CAR-T, mesothelin-directed therapies), tumor treating fields (TTFields), and combination immunotherapy approaches. Many mesothelioma centres have active trial portfolios.

alternativeTreatments.name: Tumor treating fields (TTFields)
alternativeTreatments.description: Non-invasive alternating electric fields applied to the thorax via skin transducers; approved as combination therapy with chemotherapy in some jurisdictions. Modest benefit in pleural mesothelioma in the STELLAR trial.

whySeeSpecialist: Mesothelioma diagnosis and treatment require subspecialty multidisciplinary care. Patients should be referred immediately to a designated mesothelioma centre — typically a thoracic oncology unit affiliated with a specialist mesothelioma multidisciplinary team. Surgical management (P/D, CRS+HIPEC) must be performed by experienced surgeons at high-volume centres; outcomes are strongly dependent on centre volume and experience.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a designated mesothelioma centre that performs at least 20-30 cases per year with documented outcomes. In the UK, all suspected mesothelioma is referred to a specialist multidisciplinary team. In the US, look for academic thoracic oncology centres with mesothelioma-specific programs. Surgery should be performed by thoracic surgeons or peritoneal surface oncology surgeons with documented experience in mesothelioma.
hospitalCriteria: High-volume thoracic surgery service for P/D
Peritoneal surface oncology service for CRS+HIPEC where peritoneal disease is managed
Specialist palliative care and pain medicine integration
keyFacilities: International Mesothelioma Interest Group affiliated programmes
Academic thoracic oncology and peritoneal surface oncology centres
preventionStrategies: Avoid asbestos exposure: workers in high-risk industries should use respiratory protection (P3 / N100-rated respirators), wet work practices to suppress dust, and decontamination procedures (separate work clothing, showering, monitoring of fibre counts).
Asbestos abatement should only be performed by licensed and trained professionals; do not perform DIY removal of asbestos-containing materials.
Test homes built before 1980 for asbestos-containing materials before any renovation or demolition.
Use the strictest available exposure limits — countries that have banned asbestos report falling mesothelioma rates after a 30-year latency.
Maintain occupational health surveillance for asbestos-exposed workers, including periodic chest imaging and pulmonary function testing.
Identify and treat exposed individuals' families for para-occupational risk; provide community screening where industrial contamination is documented.
Pursue policy advocacy: 70+ countries have banned asbestos use; ongoing global efforts target remaining producers (Russia, India, China, Brazil, Kazakhstan).
lifestyleModifications: Stop smoking immediately; smoking and asbestos act synergistically to increase lung cancer risk (less synergistically for mesothelioma but still important).
Attend all scheduled multidisciplinary appointments and follow-up imaging.
Disclose all asbestos exposure history to clinical team and to any solicitor handling occupational disease compensation.
Engage with palliative care and supportive care services early — survival benefit and quality-of-life improvement are documented from early integration.
Use prescribed analgesia consistently and report breakthrough pain promptly.
Maintain physical activity, nutritional intake, and psychosocial support to optimise quality of life.
Consider participation in clinical trials, particularly at specialist mesothelioma centres.

recommended: High-calorie, high-protein diet to counter cancer cachexia
Small, frequent meals if early satiety is a problem from pleural or peritoneal disease
Nutritional supplements (oral nutritional supplements, ONS) where intake is inadequate
Mediterranean-style diet emphasising vegetables, fish, and olive oil
avoid: Restrictive diets that worsen weight loss and cachexia
Unproven alternative therapies that may interact with chemotherapy or immunotherapy
Excessive iron supplementation without medical advice

exerciseGuidelines: Maintain mobility and gentle aerobic activity as tolerated. Structured exercise rehabilitation during and after treatment improves fatigue, dyspnea, and quality of life. Avoid heavy resistance training during severe thrombocytopenia or active bleeding. Pulmonary rehabilitation programs are valuable for pleural disease.
dailyManagement: Track symptoms (dyspnea, pain, weight, appetite) and report changes promptly.
Attend chemotherapy or immunotherapy infusion appointments on schedule.
Use pain medication consistently as prescribed rather than waiting for severe pain.
Manage indwelling pleural catheter drainage as taught by specialist nurses.
Attend follow-up imaging every 6-8 weeks during active treatment and every 3-4 months after treatment.
Engage with mesothelioma support groups and patient charities.
prognosis: Mesothelioma carries a serious prognosis but outcomes have improved meaningfully with modern systemic therapy. For pleural mesothelioma, median overall survival has risen from approximately 9-12 months (untreated) to 12-21 months with current first-line systemic therapy. Epithelioid histology has the best outcome (median OS 18-22 months), biphasic intermediate (12-15 months), and sarcomatoid the worst (6-8 months). The CheckMate 743 trial demonstrated 4-year overall survival of 17% with nivolumab-ipilimumab versus 10% with cisplatin-pemetrexed — a meaningful long-term survivorship that did not exist a decade ago. Peritoneal mesothelioma treated with CRS+HIPEC in selected patients achieves median OS of 30-90 months with 5-year survival approaching 50% at experienced centres. BAP1-mutated cases tend to be longer-surviving. Patients with germline BAP1 mutations have access to family screening and counselling. Long-term survivors (over 5 years) do exist, particularly with epithelioid disease, complete surgical cytoreduction, BAP1 mutation, and effective immunotherapy response. Most patients qualify for occupational disease compensation, which can ease financial impact during treatment.
recoveryTimeline: Acute treatment cycle (chemotherapy): 4-6 cycles over 12-18 weeks with response assessment by imaging after every 2-3 cycles. Immunotherapy: continued for up to 2 years or until progression or unacceptable toxicity. Surgery (P/D, CRS+HIPEC): hospital stay 5-14 days; recovery 6-12 weeks; return to function variable. Follow-up imaging every 3-4 months after treatment. Symptom management and supportive care continue lifelong.
complications: Progressive respiratory failure from pleural disease compressing the lung or invading mediastinal structures.
Pleural and pericardial effusion causing dyspnea or tamponade.
Chest wall pain that can be severe and difficult to manage.
Cachexia, weight loss, and weakness — major sources of quality-of-life impairment.
Superior vena cava syndrome from mediastinal invasion.
Spinal cord compression from vertebral metastasis.
Chemotherapy and immunotherapy toxicities — neutropenia, nephrotoxicity, immune-mediated colitis/hepatitis/pneumonitis/endocrinopathy.

supportResources.name: Mesothelioma UK
supportResources.description: UK national charity offering nursing support, information, compensation advice, and research funding for mesothelioma.

supportResources.name: Mesothelioma Applied Research Foundation (MARF)
supportResources.description: US patient-led foundation funding mesothelioma research and supporting patients and families.

supportResources.name: International Mesothelioma Interest Group (iMig)
supportResources.description: International scientific society promoting mesothelioma research and clinical excellence.

supportResources.name: Asbestos Disease Awareness Organization (ADAO)
supportResources.description: Advocacy and education organization focused on ending asbestos use and supporting affected families.

supportResources.name: American Cancer Society — Mesothelioma
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information on mesothelioma and treatment options.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lung Cancer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lung cancer (especially adenocarcinoma) can produce pleural effusion and chest wall invasion mimicking pleural mesothelioma. Distinguished by immunohistochemistry — adenocarcinoma is TTF-1 positive, CEA positive, and calretinin negative; mesothelioma is calretinin and CK5/6 positive, TTF-1 and CEA negative. Both can occur in asbestos-exposed workers.

confusedWithConditions.name: Malignant pleural effusion
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Malignant pleural effusion is a clinical syndrome — pleural fluid containing malignant cells — that can be caused by mesothelioma, metastatic carcinoma (breast, lung, ovarian), or lymphoma. Cytology and immunohistochemistry distinguish the underlying cancer.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asbestosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asbestosis is interstitial lung fibrosis from asbestos exposure, presenting with progressive dyspnea, restrictive lung function, and bibasilar reticular changes on HRCT. Mesothelioma is a malignant tumor of the mesothelial lining with characteristic pleural rind on CT. Both can occur in the same patient.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pleural plaques (asbestos)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pleural plaques are benign focal areas of fibrotic thickening on the parietal pleura caused by asbestos exposure. They are markers of exposure but do not directly progress to mesothelioma. Patients with plaques may still develop mesothelioma over time and should be monitored.

confusedWithConditions.name: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IPF is a progressive interstitial lung disease without identifiable cause. Mesothelioma is a pleural malignancy linked to asbestos. Distinguishing features include HRCT pattern (basal honeycombing in IPF vs pleural rind in mesothelioma) and presence vs absence of asbestos exposure history.

relatedConditions.name: Asbestosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Other asbestos-related disease often coexisting with mesothelioma in heavily exposed workers.

relatedConditions.name: Lung Cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Asbestos exposure synergistically increases lung cancer risk, especially in smokers; both diseases share occupational history.

relatedConditions.name: Pleural plaques
relatedConditions.relevance: Markers of asbestos exposure; identified on routine imaging; warrant ongoing monitoring even though not directly premalignant.

relatedConditions.name: Malignant pleural effusion
relatedConditions.relevance: Common manifestation of pleural mesothelioma and key target of symptom management.

relatedConditions.name: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Important differential for progressive dyspnea in an older adult with abnormal chest imaging.

faqs.question: What is mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: Mesothelioma is an aggressive cancer of the mesothelial cells lining the chest (pleura), abdomen (peritoneum), heart (pericardium), or testicle (tunica vaginalis). It is most often caused by asbestos exposure decades earlier. Pleural mesothelioma is most common, presenting with breathlessness, chest pain, and weight loss.

faqs.question: How is mesothelioma caused?
faqs.answer: Approximately 80% of mesothelioma cases are caused by asbestos exposure. Inhaled or ingested asbestos fibers lodge in mesothelial linings and cause genetic damage that, over 30-50 years, leads to malignant transformation. Other rare causes include erionite (in Cappadocia, Turkey), therapeutic radiation, and germline BAP1 mutations.

faqs.question: Is mesothelioma curable?
faqs.answer: Pleural mesothelioma is rarely curable, although long-term survival is possible in selected patients with epithelioid disease, effective immunotherapy or chemotherapy response, and lung-sparing surgery. Peritoneal mesothelioma treated with cytoreductive surgery plus HIPEC achieves median survival of 30-90 months with 5-year survival approaching 50% in well-selected cases.

faqs.question: What are the early symptoms of mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: Early symptoms are typically nonspecific: progressive breathlessness, unilateral chest pain, fatigue, and weight loss over weeks to months. Peritoneal disease causes abdominal distension and pain. Many patients are diagnosed only after a pleural effusion is investigated. Anyone with possible asbestos exposure history and these symptoms should seek prompt medical evaluation.

faqs.question: How long after asbestos exposure does mesothelioma develop?
faqs.answer: The typical latency from first asbestos exposure to clinical mesothelioma is 30-50 years, with shorter latency in heavier exposures. This long latency explains why most cases are diagnosed in older adults (median age 65-75) reflecting industrial exposures from the 1960s-1980s.

faqs.question: Who is at highest risk for mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: Highest risk is in occupations with heavy asbestos exposure: shipyard workers, insulation installers, asbestos miners, plumbers, pipefitters, electricians, boilermakers, naval personnel, brake-lining manufacturers, demolition workers, and construction trades. Family members of asbestos workers face para-occupational risk from contaminated work clothes. Men predominate roughly 4-5:1.

faqs.question: What are the treatment options for mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment for unresectable pleural disease is combination immunotherapy with nivolumab plus ipilimumab or cisplatin-pemetrexed chemotherapy. Surgery (pleurectomy/decortication) is offered to selected fit patients with epithelioid disease. Peritoneal mesothelioma is treated with cytoreductive surgery plus HIPEC. Radiation, palliative care, and pain management complete the multidisciplinary approach.

faqs.question: How is mesothelioma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires tissue biopsy — typically thoracoscopic (VATS) pleural biopsy or laparoscopic peritoneal biopsy. Immunohistochemistry shows characteristic positive markers (calretinin, CK5/6, WT1, D2-40) and negative markers (CEA, TTF-1, claudin 4). BAP1 loss and MTAP loss support malignancy. CT, MRI, and PET-CT assess extent of disease.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate of mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: Pleural mesothelioma median overall survival is 12-21 months with modern therapy; 4-year overall survival is 17% with nivolumab-ipilimumab versus 10% with chemotherapy alone in the CheckMate 743 trial. Peritoneal mesothelioma with CRS+HIPEC achieves median survival of 30-90 months in selected patients. Epithelioid histology has the best prognosis; sarcomatoid the worst.

faqs.question: Can mesothelioma be prevented?
faqs.answer: Yes, primarily through eliminating asbestos exposure. Countries that have banned asbestos report falling mesothelioma rates after a 30-year latency. Workers in high-risk industries should use proper respiratory protection (P3/N100), wet work practices, and decontamination. Asbestos abatement should only be done by licensed professionals. Avoid DIY removal of asbestos materials.

faqs.question: Is mesothelioma the same as lung cancer?
faqs.answer: No. Lung cancer arises from lung epithelium and forms tumors within the lung tissue. Mesothelioma arises from the mesothelial cells lining the pleura, peritoneum, or other body cavities. Both can be caused by asbestos exposure and can occur together, but they are distinct diseases with different treatments.

faqs.question: What is BAP1 mutation in mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: BAP1 is a tumor suppressor gene. Loss of BAP1 occurs in 60-70% of mesotheliomas. Germline mutations in BAP1 cause BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome with elevated mesothelioma, uveal melanoma, and kidney cancer risk. Germline testing is offered to younger patients, peritoneal disease, or family history of BAP1 cancers — important for family screening.

faqs.question: Does immunotherapy work for mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Nivolumab plus ipilimumab is now first-line therapy for unresectable pleural mesothelioma, showing improved overall survival (18.1 vs 14.1 months) versus cisplatin-pemetrexed in the CheckMate 743 trial. Benefit is particularly strong in non-epithelioid disease. Pembrolizumab is also approved in combination with chemotherapy. Nivolumab monotherapy works as second-line therapy after chemotherapy.

faqs.question: What is HIPEC?
faqs.answer: Hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC) is heated chemotherapy (cisplatin or mitomycin C) circulated through the abdomen for 60-90 minutes after cytoreductive surgery removes visible tumor. It is standard for peritoneal mesothelioma at specialist centres and produces median survival of 30-90 months in selected patients.

faqs.question: Can mesothelioma run in families?
faqs.answer: Most mesothelioma is sporadic and related to asbestos exposure. Germline BAP1 mutations produce familial clusters with elevated risk of mesothelioma, uveal melanoma, kidney cancer, and other tumors. Genetic counseling and testing should be offered to patients with personal or family history suggesting BAP1 syndrome.

faqs.question: Should I get compensation for asbestos exposure?
faqs.answer: Most mesothelioma patients with documented asbestos exposure qualify for occupational disease compensation. In the UK, government industrial injuries benefits and the Diffuse Mesothelioma Payment Scheme provide compensation. In the US, asbestos trust funds and personal injury litigation exist. Discuss with medical social workers, mesothelioma charities, and specialist solicitors early after diagnosis.

faqs.question: What is pleurectomy/decortication?
faqs.answer: Pleurectomy/decortication (P/D) is a lung-sparing surgical procedure for pleural mesothelioma that removes all visible tumor on parietal and visceral pleura while preserving the lung. It is the preferred surgical approach over extrapleural pneumonectomy following the MARS2 trial. Performed only at specialist centres.

faqs.question: Can mesothelioma develop without asbestos exposure?
faqs.answer: Roughly 20% of mesothelioma cases occur without documented asbestos exposure. Other causes include erionite mineral fibers (Cappadocia, Turkey), therapeutic radiation, germline BAP1 mutations, and rare environmental exposures. Some cases remain unexplained. Mesothelioma without asbestos exposure should prompt genetic testing for BAP1 and other predisposition genes.

faqs.question: How is pleural mesothelioma different from peritoneal mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: Pleural mesothelioma (around 80%) arises in chest cavity and causes breathlessness, chest pain, and effusion; treated with chemotherapy, immunotherapy, and selected surgery. Peritoneal mesothelioma (10-20%) arises in abdominal cavity and causes pain, ascites, and distension; treated with cytoreductive surgery plus HIPEC at specialist centres. Peritoneal has better prognosis with CRS+HIPEC than pleural.

faqs.question: Is talc safe given concerns about asbestos contamination?
faqs.answer: Pure pharmaceutical-grade talc used in pleurodesis is asbestos-free and considered safe. Historical concerns about cosmetic talc contamination with asbestos have led to litigation in some jurisdictions, but pharmaceutical talc undergoes stringent purity testing. Talc pleurodesis remains a standard palliative procedure for mesothelioma.

faqs.question: What clinical trials are available for mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: Active trial programmes evaluate novel immunotherapy combinations, mesothelin-targeted CAR-T cells, tumor treating fields (TTFields), and small-molecule agents targeting BAP1-deficient cells. All mesothelioma patients should be considered for trial enrollment, particularly at designated mesothelioma centres. clinicaltrials.gov and major mesothelioma charity websites maintain trial listings.

metaDescription: Mesothelioma: asbestos-related cancer of pleura or peritoneum. Symptoms, diagnosis, nivolumab/ipilimumab, pleurectomy, HIPEC reviewed by aihealz.
